metadata.summary: Harden Kubernetes workloads with Pod Security Standards, container securityContext, dedicated ServiceAccounts with minimal RBAC, NetworkPolicies (default-deny), and secrets management best practices.

content.title: Kubernetes Security Hardening
content.summary: Kubernetes workload security requires five layers: Pod Security Standards (enforce restricted namespace labels), container securityContext (non-root, read-only root filesystem, drop ALL capabilities), dedicated ServiceAccounts with minimal RBAC, NetworkPolicies enforcing default-deny with explicit allow rules, and secrets never stored in ConfigMaps or environment variables — use secretKeyRef or an external vault.

why.p: Kubernetes defaults are permissive: pods run as root, can escalate privileges, read the default ServiceAccount token (which has cluster-wide permissions in many setups), and communicate with any other pod. A compromised container in a cluster without security hardening becomes an attack path to the Kubernetes API, adjacent services, and cloud metadata APIs. Pod Security Standards (replacing the deprecated PodSecurityPolicy) enforce baseline/restricted profiles at namespace admission time. NetworkPolicies enforce zero-trust pod-to-pod communication. Together they implement defence-in-depth: a compromised pod cannot pivot to the database or the API server.

list.item: Deploying any production workload to Kubernetes.
Auditing an existing Deployment for security misconfigurations.
Setting up a new namespace for production workloads.
Development namespaces with throwaway workloads where security overhead slows iteration — use baseline PSS at minimum.
Legacy applications that genuinely require root (e.g., some database init scripts) — use securityContext.runAsUser: 0 explicitly rather than omitting it, and document the exception.
Namespace-level PSS enforcement on namespaces with existing workloads before validating compatibility — start with warn mode.

section.name: pod-security-standards
section.p: Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PodSecurityPolicy in Kubernetes 1.25+. Three profiles: Privileged (unrestricted), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalations), Restricted (strongly hardened). Apply via namespace labels. Three modes: enforce (reject violating pods), audit (log but allow), warn (warn but allow). Production namespaces MUST use enforce: restricted.

rule.statement: Label every production namespace with pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted. Use warn and audit modes in staging to catch violations before they block production deploys.
rule.rationale: The enforce label blocks admission of any pod that does not comply with the restricted profile — no root containers, no privilege escalation, no host namespaces. Without enforcement, security context fields are optional and developers omit them under deadline pressure.

example.title: Namespace with restricted PSS
example.code: apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: production labels: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version: latest pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn-version: latest pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit-version: latest

section.name: container-security-context
section.p: securityContext fields at container and pod level implement the mandatory controls required by the restricted PSS profile. All six fields must be set: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation, capabilities.drop ALL, and seccompProfile RuntimeDefault.

rule.statement: Every production container MUST set runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser >= 1000, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: [ALL], and seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault on the pod spec. Writable paths (tmp, cache) must use emptyDir volumes.
rule.rationale: runAsNonRoot prevents a container compromise from immediately having UID 0 on the host. readOnlyRootFilesystem prevents malware writing to /usr/bin or /etc. Dropping ALL capabilities removes file ownership change, raw socket access, and other Linux privilege mechanisms. RuntimeDefault seccomp filters ~300 syscalls not needed by normal applications.

example.title: Container and pod security context
example.code:
spec: securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true runAsUser: 1000 runAsGroup: 1000 fsGroup: 1000 seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
containers: - name: api securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false readOnlyRootFilesystem: true capabilities: drop: - ALL
volumeMounts: - name: tmp mountPath: /tmp - name: cache mountPath: /app/cache
volumes: - name: tmp emptyDir: sizeLimit: 100Mi - name: cache emptyDir: sizeLimit: 500Mi

checklist.check: Only necessary capabilities added back (usually none).

section.name: service-account-rbac
section.p: The default ServiceAccount in most namespaces has a mounted API token with cluster-wide read access. Applications that do not call the Kubernetes API should have automountServiceAccountToken: false. Applications that do need API access must use a dedicated ServiceAccount with a minimal Role.

rule.statement: Create a dedicated ServiceAccount per application. Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on the ServiceAccount (or pod spec) for applications that do not call the Kubernetes API. Use a Role and RoleBinding with minimal verbs — never ClusterRole for namespace-scoped workloads.
rule.rationale: If a container is compromised, the mounted token grants the attacker whatever permissions the ServiceAccount has. The default ServiceAccount often has broad read access across the cluster. automountServiceAccountToken: false means no token to steal.

example.title: Dedicated ServiceAccount with minimal RBAC
example.code: apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: api namespace: production automountServiceAccountToken: false --- apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: name: api namespace: production rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["configmaps"] verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"] --- apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: RoleBinding metadata: name: api namespace: production subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount name: api namespace: production roleRef: kind: Role name: api apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

rule.statement: Never store secrets in ConfigMaps or as plain environment variable values in the Deployment manifest. Always use secretKeyRef / secretRef for environment variables, or mount secrets as files. In production, use an External Secrets Operator or Vault to keep secrets out of etcd entirely.
rule.rationale: ConfigMaps are not encrypted at rest by default; anyone with ConfigMap read access sees the secret. Manifest-embedded plaintext secrets leak into git history and CI logs. External secrets operators sync secrets from a vault at pod start time so only an encrypted reference is stored in Kubernetes.

example.title: Secret via secretKeyRef
example.code: env: - name: DATABASE_URL valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: api-secrets key: database-url - name: SECRET_KEY valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: api-secrets key: secret-key

example.title: Secret in ConfigMap or plain env
example.code: # ConfigMap - DO NOT store secrets here apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap data: DATABASE_URL: "postgres://user:password@host:5432/db" --- # Or plain env value in Deployment - DO NOT do this env: - name: DATABASE_URL value: "postgres://user:password@host:5432/db"
example.note: Both patterns leak credentials to anyone with ConfigMap/Deployment read access, and into kubectl describe output visible in cluster logs.

checklist.check: Secrets not hardcoded in manifests or ConfigMaps.
External secrets operator or vault used in production.

section.name: network-policies
section.p: By default all pods in a Kubernetes cluster can communicate with all other pods, including pods in other namespaces. NetworkPolicy implements zero-trust at the pod level: a default-deny policy blocks all traffic, explicit rules allow only what is necessary. DNS (UDP 53 to kube-dns) must always be explicitly allowed in egress rules.

rule.statement: Apply a default-deny NetworkPolicy to every production namespace. Then add explicit ingress rules (from ingress controller namespace) and egress rules (to database, cache, DNS). Never use a wildcard namespaceSelector in a production NetworkPolicy.
rule.rationale: A default-deny posture means a new pod that should not receive traffic from the database cannot accidentally receive it if someone misconfigures the deployment selector. Explicit egress rules prevent a compromised pod from exfiltrating data to an external IP.

example.title: Full NetworkPolicy with default-deny
example.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: api-network-policy namespace: production spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: api policyTypes: - Ingress - Egress
ingress: # Allow from ingress controller - from: - namespaceSelector: matchLabels: kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000
# Allow from other api pods (for health checks) - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: api ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8000
egress: # Allow to database - to: - podSelector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: postgres ports: - protocol: TCP port: 5432
# Allow to Redis - to: - podSelector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: redis ports: - protocol: TCP port: 6379
# Allow DNS (required) - to: - namespaceSelector: {} podSelector: matchLabels: k8s-app: kube-dns ports: - protocol: UDP port: 53

checklist.check: NetworkPolicy denies all ingress by default (no ingress rules = deny all ingress).
NetworkPolicy denies all egress by default.
Explicit ingress rules for required traffic.
DNS egress rule present (UDP 53 to kube-dns).
NetworkPolicy verified: kubectl get networkpolicy -n namespace.

list.item: Kyverno — Kubernetes-native policy engine, YAML policies, generates resources, validates admission.
OPA Gatekeeper — Rego-based policies for complex admission control.
Polaris — best practices validation, built-in checks, dashboard, score > 80% is good baseline.
kubectl apply --dry-run=server — validate manifests against admission webhooks before apply.
kubeconform — offline YAML schema validation, fast in CI.

checklist.check: No secrets in ConfigMaps or plain env values.
